findings.reachability: Reachable by any caller that supplies an empty or null idp_certificates array; when idp_certificates is [] or null, the for-loop over `ref = idp_certificates || []` never executes, the code falls through to `cb_wf(new Error(...))` — so this specific path is actually an error. However, when idp_certificates is falsy and ignore_signature is true (line 589), the assertion is accepted completely unsigned.
findings.justification: When `options.ignore_signature` is true (a caller-controlled flag), the function at line 589 immediately calls `cb_wf(null, DOMParser.parseFromString(result))` with zero cryptographic verification, accepting any forged SAML assertion; this flag flows directly from the options object controlled by the application developer and can be set per-request via `request_options` passed to `post_assert`/`get_assert`.

detection_pattern.description: Catches SAML libraries that accept a caller-supplied ignore_signature flag which completely skips cryptographic verification of the assertion, enabling acceptance of forged tokens.

findings.reachability: Reachable by any attacker who can deliver a SAML Response (e.g., MITM or stolen assertion replay) to the SP's assertion consumer endpoint.
findings.justification: `parse_response_header` parses the `InResponseTo` attribute from the SAML Response and returns it in `response_header.in_response_to`, but nowhere in `_assert` or `parse_authn_response` is this value validated against a stored nonce/ID of a request that was actually issued; the library provides no replay-prevention or request-binding, making it susceptible to SAML assertion replay attacks.

detection_pattern.description: Flags SAML response parsers that read InResponseTo but do not cross-check the value against a persisted set of request IDs, leaving the SP open to assertion replay.

findings.reachability: Reachable by any IdP-controlled or forged SAML Assertion; if signature verification is bypassed (ignore_signature or forged cert), an attacker sets an Attribute Name of `__proto__` or `constructor` in the assertion.
findings.justification: At line 480, `assertion_attributes[attribute_name] = ...` writes IdP-supplied attribute names as plain object keys with no sanitization; when `attribute_name` is `__proto__`, this pollutes `Object.prototype` because `{}['__proto__']` targets the prototype in older V8/Node versions (pre-Node 12 hardening); even in newer runtimes, `assertion_attributes['constructor'] = [...]` clobbers the constructor property, and the object is then merged via `_.extend(user, ...)` at line 670, spreading the pollution to the returned `user` object.

detection_pattern.description: Catches code that assigns parsed/user-controlled string keys directly to plain-object properties without sanitizing __proto__ or constructor, enabling prototype pollution from attacker-supplied data.

findings.reachability: Reachable by any attacker who can deliver a signed (or unverified) SAML Assertion that omits the Conditions element.
findings.justification: The `Conditions` element check at line 628 is guarded by `if (conditions != null)` — if the element is absent, both the timing check (NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter) and the audience restriction check are silently skipped, meaning a valid-signature assertion with no Conditions element will be accepted for any SP audience and at any time.

detection_pattern.description: Flags SAML parsers that treat the Conditions element as optional; a missing Conditions element should be an error, not a pass-through, because it carries the validity window and audience restriction.

findings.reachability: Reachable when IdP sends an assertion with an AudienceRestriction element that contains zero Audience children.
findings.justification: At line 648, audience validation only triggers `if (audiences.length > 0)`; an AudienceRestriction element with no `<Audience>` children is silently accepted, meaning a stolen assertion from another SP can be replayed here if the attacker strips the Audience sub-elements while keeping the AudienceRestriction wrapper, which may remain valid under the original signature depending on the C14N scope.

detection_pattern.description: Catches SAML audience validation that accepts an AudienceRestriction element with zero Audience children as valid, which can permit cross-SP assertion replay.

findings.location: check_saml_signature line 353; _assert line 806; multiple DOMParser calls
findings.reachability: Reachable by any caller that submits a SAML Response (unauthenticated endpoint in most SAML SP deployments).
findings.justification: `new xmldom.DOMParser().parseFromString(xml)` is called on attacker-controlled XML at multiple points (lines 353, 590, 606); the `@xmldom/xmldom` parser's XXE behavior depends on its version and configuration — older versions did not disable external entities by default, so if the deployed version resolves DOCTYPE entity references the file is vulnerable to blind XXE; no entity-disabling options are passed to the DOMParser constructor.

detection_pattern.description: Catches xmldom DOMParser instantiations without explicit entity-disabling options that parse attacker-controlled XML, potentially enabling XXE if the installed xmldom version resolves external entities.

findings.reachability: Reachable by any caller that controls the IdentityProvider.sso_logout_url option; in deployments where IdP metadata is fetched dynamically or from a database, an SSRF-adjacent open redirect results.
findings.justification: `url.parse(identity_provider.sso_logout_url, true)` at line 862 parses the URL supplied in IdP options, then appends query parameters and calls `url.format(uri)` to produce the redirect destination; if `sso_logout_url` is not validated against an allowlist, an attacker who can influence IdP configuration (e.g., multi-tenant apps) can cause the SP to redirect users to an arbitrary URL.

detection_pattern.description: Flags SAML libraries that build redirect URLs directly from IdP-supplied SSO URLs without allowlist validation, enabling open redirect or SSRF in multi-tenant deployments.
